title: Actions Runner Architecture
description: Deep dive into GitHub Actions Runner internals: job scheduling, self-hosted runner architecture, autoscaling with ephemeral runners, and network/security considerations at scale.

sections.title: Runner Architecture
sections.description: Each runner instance runs a Listener process that polls for jobs and a Worker process that executes one job at a time. The listener manages keep-alive, job assignment, and graceful shutdown.
sections.codeBlock:
# Runner directory structure
.
├── bin/
│   ├── Runner.Listener   # polls GitHub for jobs
│   └── Runner.Worker     # executes job steps
├── .runner               # registration credentials
├── .credentials          # OAuth token (scoped)
├── .env                  # environment overrides
├── _diag/                # diagnostic logs
├── _work/                # job working directories
│   └── <repo>/
│       └── <run-id>/
│           └── <job-name>/
# logs flow: _diag/procfilename.log-<pid>.log

sections.title: Job Scheduling
sections.description: The Actions backend dispatches jobs to runners based on labels, concurrency limits, and queue depth. Each job gets an isolated working directory and fresh environment with context injection.
sections.codeBlock:
# Job dispatch flow
# 1. Workflow trigger → check-runs queued
# 2. Matrix expansion → N individual job runs
# 3. Label matching: runs-on: [self-hosted, linux, x64]
# 4. Runner picks job via long-poll
# 5. Worker downloads job payload
# 6. Steps execute sequentially
# 7. Logs streamed via chunked upload
# 8. Artifacts uploaded → job marked complete
# Runner labels define routing:
#   runs-on: [self-hosted, linux, gpu]
#   → only runners with ALL three labels qualify

sections.title: Self-Hosted Runners
sections.description: Self-hosted runners give you control over hardware, installed software, and network access. They register at the repository, organization, or enterprise level and can be persistent or ephemeral.
sections.codeBlock:
# Register a self-hosted runner
# https://github.com/<org>/<repo>/settings/actions/runners
./config.sh --url https://github.com/<org> \
  --token AAAAAAA --name runner-prod-01 \
  --labels prod,linux,x64 --ephemeral

# Ephemeral mode: runs one job then exits
# Replace token per registration
# systemd service recommended:
./svc.sh install
./svc.sh start

# Enterprise-level registration:
./config.sh --url https://github.com/enterprises/<enterprise> \
  --token AAAAAAA --ephemeral

sections.title: Autoscaling
sections.description: Scale sets using the actions/actions-runner-controller or cloud-native auto-scaling groups dynamically provision ephemeral runners based on job queue depth. Scale to zero when idle saves cost.
sections.codeBlock:
# KubeRnetes ARC (Actions Runner Controller)
apiVersion: actions.summerwind.dev/v1alpha1
kind: RunnerDeployment
metadata:
  name: example-runner
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    spec:
      organization: my-org
      labels:
        - linux
        - x64
      ephemeral: true
---
apiVersion: actions.summerwind.dev/v1alpha1
kind: HorizontalRunnerAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: example-autoscaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: example-runner
  minReplicas: 0
  maxReplicas: 20
  scaleUpTriggers:
    - githubEvent:
        workflowJob: {}
      duration: 30m

sections.title: Network & Security
sections.description: Self-hosted runners require outbound HTTPS access to api.github.com and blob storage. For private networking, use VPC injection, NAT gateways, and OIDC trust for cloud provider access.

details: The Actions runner is a .NET application with two primary processes: Runner.Listener handles registration, heartbeats, and job polling via long-lived HTTP connections to GitHub's API. Runner.Worker is spawned per job to execute steps in an isolated _work directory with injected environment variables, secrets, and job context. Self-hosted runners extend this model with custom hardware, software pre-installation, and private network access. The ARC (Actions Runner Controller) project brings Kubernetes-native autoscaling with ephemeral pods that register, execute exactly one job, and terminate — eliminating credential leakage risk and ensuring clean state. For enterprise deployments, runner redundancy, health probes, and deployment group labeling are critical for reliability. Network security requires egress-only access to GitHub endpoints and OIDC-based workload identity federation to avoid long-lived cloud credentials on the runner filesystem.
cheatSheet:
Core Commands

  • ./config.sh --url https://github.com/org --token $TOKEN — register self-hosted runner.
  • runs-on: [self-hosted, linux, x64, gpu] — target specific runners with labels.
  • ACTIONS_RUNNER_DEBUG: true — enable runner-level debug logging.

Staff+ Patterns

  • Use ephemeral runners for security: each job gets a fresh VM that's destroyed after use. Prevents credential leakage between jobs.
  • Set up auto-scaling with actions/actions-runner-controller (ARC) on Kubernetes — scale to zero when idle, scale up on demand.

Watch Outs

  • Self-hosted runners on the same network as production are a security risk. Isolate in a separate VPC with outbound-only access to GitHub.
  • Runner registration tokens expire after 1 hour. Use the actions/runner-registration action for automated provisioning.

Interview Angle

Netflix L6 asks about runner architecture: how does the Actions coordinator dispatch jobs? Discuss polling vs push model, runner group isolation, and the trade-off between hosted (no ops, cost per minute) and self-hosted (free compute, ops cost).
